era_context: The late Roman Empire under Eastern Emperor Valens and Western Emperor Gratian. The empire was experiencing increasing pressure from Germanic peoples, particularly the Goths, who were migrating southward from the Danube region. The Arian-Nicene Christian controversy remained a major source of religious and political tension. This year marks the period immediately before the catastrophic Battle of Adrianople (378 CE), during which Gothic refugees were being settled within Roman territory.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Non-Roman regions (Persia, China, India, sub-Saharan Africa) have limited documentation in surviving sources for this specific year.

events.title: Gothic refugees seek settlement within the Roman Empire
events.description: In 377 CE, large numbers of Goths and other Germanic peoples, fleeing pressure from the Huns in Central Asia, sought refuge within the Roman Empire. The Eastern Roman Emperor Valens, facing military pressure and seeking to replenish his armies, agreed to settle these refugees as foederati (allied troops) within the empire, particularly in the Balkans. The settlement process was chaotic and poorly managed. Roman officials allegedly exploited the Goths, providing inadequate food supplies and mistreating them. These tensions would eventually lead to the Gothic uprising and the Battle of Adrianople in 378 CE. The 377 CE settlement represents a critical moment in the transformation of the late Roman Empire, as it marked the beginning of large-scale Germanic settlement within Roman territory.
key_figures: Lupicinus (Roman general, allegedly mistreated the Goths)

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (written late 4th century, covers the settlement)
Jordanes, Getica (written 6th century, covers Gothic perspective)

events.certainty_note: The Gothic settlement in 377 CE is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. Ammianus Marcellinus provides detailed coverage.
cross_references: Led to Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)
Part of broader barbarian migrations of the late 4th century

events.title: Valens's military preparations and frontier defense
events.description: In 377 CE, Valens was preparing for military campaigns against the Goths and other barbarian groups along the Danube frontier. He was consolidating troops and resources for what he believed would be a decisive military campaign. However, his military preparations were hampered by the need to manage the settlement of Gothic refugees and by the complexity of the frontier situation. Valens's military strategy would ultimately prove inadequate, leading to the catastrophic defeat at Adrianople in 378 CE.
events.certainty_note: Valens's military preparations in 377 CE are attested in sources, but specific details are less comprehensive than for 378 CE.
cross_references: Led to Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)

events.title: Religious tensions under Valens's Arian policies
events.description: In 377 CE, the Eastern Roman Empire under Valens was experiencing significant religious tensions. Valens, unlike his predecessor Constantine, favored Arian Christianity over Nicene orthodoxy. His religious policies favored Arian bishops and suppressed Nicene Christians, creating deep divisions within the Christian community. These religious tensions would persist and eventually be resolved (in favor of Nicene orthodoxy) under Theodosius I, who would issue the Edict of Thessalonica in 380 CE. The 377 CE religious situation reflects the broader instability of the late 4th-century empire.
key_figures: Gregory of Nazianzus (Nicene theologian, critic of Valens)

sources.name: Gregory of Nazianzus, Orations (written late 4th century, criticizes Valens's religious policies)
Socrates Scholasticus, Ecclesiastical History (written early 5th century)
Historiographic consensus on late 4th-century Christian disputes

events.certainty_note: Valens's Arian sympathies and the resulting religious tensions are well-attested in contemporary sources, particularly the writings of Gregory of Nazianzus.
cross_references: Led to Edict of Thessalonica (380 CE) under Theodosius I
Led to First Council of Constantinople (381 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Valens as a weak or incompetent emperor has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship. Some historians argue that Valens was a capable administrator and military commander, and that his defeat at Adrianople was due to specific tactical errors and unfavorable circumstances rather than general incompetence. Additionally, the narrative of Valens's Arian policies as uniformly oppressive has been complicated; some scholars note that Valens's religious policies were more pragmatic and less uniformly hostile to Nicene Christians than sometimes portrayed.
historiographic_note: The year 377 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire, particularly regarding the Gothic settlement and Valens's religious policies. Ammianus Marcellinus provides detailed coverage of the military and political situation. However, sources are predominantly written from the Roman perspective. The Gothic perspective is less detailed. Information on non-Roman regions (Persia, China, India, sub-Saharan Africa) is sparse or absent from surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The Gothic settlement and mistreatment of refugees in 377 CE led to the Battle of Adrianople in 378 CE.
Valens's Arian policies in 377 CE led to Theodosius I's enforcement of Nicene orthodoxy via the Edict of Thessalonica in 380 CE.
Valens's military preparations in 377 CE led to the Battle of Adrianople in 378 CE.
